steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for a generalization about brown seaweed. A generalization draws a broad conclusion about a category based on specific examples. The notes give two specific brown seaweeds (macrokelp and ma-konbu) that both trigger umami, so the answer must state a general claim about brown seaweed as a type.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Macrokelp is a type of brown seaweed, as is ma-konbu.: Only identifies the two samples as brown seaweeds. Makes no claim about what brown seaweed does — this is a classification, not a generalization. ✗
  • Choice B — In a research study, participants tasted samples of macrokelp and ma-konbu, two types of brown seaweed.: Describes what happened in the study. Stays locked to the specific experiment rather than drawing any conclusion about the category. ✗
  • Choice C — Participants in a study rated the umami intensity of various seaweeds.: Describes the participants' action. Says nothing about what brown seaweed actually does, and misrepresents the notes (only brown seaweeds were tested, not "various seaweeds"). ✗
  • Choice D — Some types of brown seaweed trigger umami flavor in human taste buds.: Moves from the two specific samples to a claim about brown seaweed as a category. "Some types" appropriately hedges the generalization to what the data supports. ✓
That matches Choice D — Some types of brown seaweed trigger umami flavor in human taste buds..
